original_abstract:
This paper re-frames attack detection in the smart grid as a statistical learning challenge, addressing various attack scenarios where measurements are gathered in either batch or online settings. By treating detection as a binary classification problem, we utilize machine learning algorithms to distinguish between secure and compromised measurements. The proposed framework is designed to leverage existing prior knowledge of system topology while overcoming the constraints imposed by the sparse nature of power grid data.

We investigate a diverse suite of batch and online learning methods—ranging from supervised and semi-supervised models to decision- and feature-level fusion strategies—to effectively model the detection environment. Furthermore, we analyze the statistical and geometric relationships between attack vectors and learning algorithms, specifically to identify "unobservable" attacks that typically bypass conventional residual-based checks. 

The effectiveness of these algorithms is rigorously tested on multiple IEEE benchmark systems. Our experimental results demonstrate that machine learning approaches significantly outperform traditional attack detection methods based on state vector estimation. In particular, we identify performance phase transitions in Support Vector Machines (SVMs) that correspond to critical system thresholds, suggesting that statistical learning offers a robust defense against sophisticated data injection threats.
humanized_abstract:
A novel approach to attack detection problem in the smart grid based on statistical learning theory is proposed in this paper for batch and online measurements, and attacks considered include tampering attacks and false data injection attacks. Using the machine learning method to judge whether the measured data are legitimate or not and thereby detect whether the grid has been attacked or not based on classification from normal to attacking, this paper considers not only the structural feature of the power system by utilizing the prior knowledge of its topology but also deals with the scarcity of grid data.

This research looks into several batch and online learning methods, including supervised and semi-supervised learning models as well as decision- and feature-level fusion methods to understand the detection environment. We analyze the statistics and geometry related to various types of attacks and learning algorithms to determine the types of attacks that are difficult to be discovered, especially “unobservable” attacks that are usually missed by the common residual-based based detection methods.

The performance of our algorithms is rigorously verified by applying them to multiple IEEE benchmark systems. By contrasting the performance of various machine learning techniques with standard attack detection approaches that utilize state vector estimation, we make several important contributions: First, our results for several learning approaches indicate that they significantly outperform such approaches based on state vector estimation, hence providing empirical evidence that a new class of defense mechanisms have arrived based on statistical learning methods. Secondly, we clearly identify performance phase transitions in the learning approach we exemplify by SVMs occurring right at the critical values of the system parameters, which underscores the value of such learning methods for defeating very sophisticated data injection attacks.
